When presented with a simple, rule-driven, one-page binder proposal, users respond positively and approve it when it matches their preference for short, concise checklists. When the user finds a checklist to be 'plain enough,' they accept it without further request and are satisfied with the concise, rule-driven format.

To send a firm complaint about a damaged item, verify all facts before drafting and provide a clear, organized, and hard-to-ignore draft with a version for records; to make a paper-based system description actionable and unambiguous: use a mail-ready version with specific, filled-in dates and exact damage details to prevent disputes; explicitly state retention of photos, receipts, and requirement for written replies only to enforce accountability.

To create a usable, printable paper checklist: design a single-page table with clear columns (Date, Item, Category, Note, What I did); place the four category options (ACT / KEEP short term / KEEP long term / WATCH) directly in the Category column; add a short reminder (scam/duplicate/doesn’t match my records) in the header or footer; avoid explanatory text or example lines to prevent clutter and crossing out.
